subject: Physics
solution:
Young's modulus: $Y = \frac{\sigma}{\epsilon} = \frac{F/A}{\Delta L/L}$.
When the same force is applied and both wires stretch by the same extension $\Delta L$, the ratio of Young's moduli is:
$\frac{Y_A}{Y_B} = \frac{F/A_A}{\Delta L/L_A} \times \frac{\Delta L/L_B}{F/A_B} = \frac{A_B}{A_A} \times \frac{L_A}{L_B}$
$=\frac{4.5 \times 10^{-5}}{3.0 \times 10^{-5}} \times \frac{6.0}{5.4} = 1.5 \times \frac{10}{9} = \frac{5}{3}$.
Therefore $x : 3 = 5 : 3$, giving $x = 5$.

subject: Physics
solution:
The relations of Young's modulus, Y, bulk modulus, K, the modulus of rigidity, η and Poisson's ratio, σ are given as

Y=3K(1-2σ) …1 and 

Y=2η1+σ …2

From equation 1 and 2, we have

⇒2η1+σ=3K1-2σ

⇒σ=3K-2η6K+2η

subject: Physics
solution:
The external force or tension F acting on the wire is its own weight. It is given by

 F= mg=4 kg×104 m s-2=10 N

The formula to calculate the Young's modulus of the material of the wire is given by

Y= FA∆LL= FL∆LA..........................(1)

where, A is the cross-sectional area, L is the original length and ∆L is the elongation of the wire.

Substitute the given values of the known parameters into equation (1) and solve to calculate the required elongation of the wire.

2×1011 N/m2= 10 N×6 m∆L×3 mm2×1 m2106 mm2∆L= 10 N×6 m2×1011 N/m2×3 mm2×1 m2106 mm2= 0.0001 m×103 mm1 m= 0.1 mm

subject: Physics
solution: $\begin{aligned} & 3=\mathrm{K}(\mathrm{a}-\ell) \\ & 2=\mathrm{K}(\mathrm{b}-\ell) \\ & \mathrm{T}=\mathrm{K}(3 \mathrm{a}-2 \mathrm{~b}-\ell) \\ & \mathrm{T}=\mathrm{K}(3(\mathrm{a}-\ell)-2(\mathrm{~b}-\ell) \\ & =\mathrm{K}\left[3\left(\frac{3}{\mathrm{~K}}\right)-2\left(\frac{2}{\mathrm{~K}}\right)\right] \\ & =9-4 \\ & =5 \mathrm{~N}\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
solution:
The formula for Young's modulus is given by

Y=FA∆ll=FlA∆l

From above equation, it follows that

Δl=FlAY   ...1

Again, volume of the wire is given by

V=Al⇒l=VA

Hence, from equation (1),

∆l=FVA2Y   ...2

As, Y and V is the same for both the wires,

∆l∝F A2

Hence, for two wires, it can be written that

∆l1∆l2=F1 A12×A22 F2   ...3

As ∆l1=∆l2, from equation (3), it follows that

F1 A22=F2 A12⇒F1 F2=A12 A22=412=16

subject: Physics
solution: Shearing strain is created along the side surface of the punched disk. Note that the forces exerted on the disk are exerted along the circumference of the disk, and the total force exerted on its center only. Let us assume that the shearing stress along the side surface of the disk is uniform, then $$ \begin{aligned} \mathrm{F}>& \int_{\text {surface }} \mathrm{dF}_{\max }=\int_{\text {surface }} \sigma_{\max } \mathrm{dA}=\sigma_{\max } \int_{\text {surface }} \mathrm{dA} \\ &=\int \sigma_{\max } \cdot \mathrm{A}=\sigma_{\max } \cdot 2 \pi\left(\frac{\mathrm{D}}{2}\right) \mathrm{h} \\ &=3.5 \times 10^8 \times\left(\frac{1}{2} \times 10^{-2}\right) \times 0.3 \times 10^{-2} \times 2 \pi \\ &=3.297 \times 10^4 \simeq 3.3 \times 10^4 \mathrm{~N} \end{aligned} $$

solution:
The formula to calculate the Young's modulus of the material is given by

Y=stressstrain=stress∆lL   ...1

The formula to calculate the potential energy U stored into the wire is given by

U=12×stress×strain×volume=12×stress×∆lL×A×L= 12×stress×A×∆l   ...2

From equation (1) and equation (2), it can be written that

U=12×Y×∆l2L×A   ...3

Substitute the values of the known parameters into equation (3) and solve to calculate the required cross-sectional area of the wire.

80 J=12×2.0×1011 Nm-2×0.02 m220 m×A⇒A= 2×80 J×202.0×1011×4×10-4 Nm-1=4×10-5 m2×106 mm21 m2= 40 mm2

subject: Physics
solution:
Given that the wires have equal cross-sectional area $A$ and are under the same tension $F$, the elongation $\Delta L$ is given by:

$\Delta L = \dfrac{F L}{A Y}$

Since the elongations in both wires are equal ($\Delta L_A = \Delta L_B$):

$\dfrac{F L_A}{A Y_A} = \dfrac{F L_B}{A Y_B}$

$\dfrac{L_A}{Y_A} = \dfrac{L_B}{Y_B}$

$L_B = L_A \times \dfrac{Y_B}{Y_A}$

Substituting the given values $L_A = 2.2$ m and $\dfrac{Y_A}{Y_B} = \dfrac{20}{11}$:

$L_B = 2.2 \times \dfrac{11}{20}$

$L_B = \dfrac{24.2}{20} = 1.21$ m

Answer: $1.21$

subject: Physics
solution:
With rise in temperature, Young’s modulus of  elasticity decreases due to the increase in atomic 
vibrations which leads to decrease in atomic forces. The Young's modulus is a measure of stiffness of the material. With rise in temperature, the length of the material increases which decreases its stiffness.

subject: Physics
solution:
Let the maximum mass that can be added to the pan be $m$.

The breaking stress for both wires is $\sigma_{max} = 12 \times 10^8 \text{ N/m}^2$.

For the upper wire:
Area of cross-section, $A_u = 0.008 \text{ cm}^2 = 8 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}^2$
Maximum tension the upper wire can withstand is:
$T_{u,max} = \sigma_{max} \times A_u = (12 \times 10^8) \times (8 \times 10^{-7}) = 960 \text{ N}$
The total mass supported by the upper wire is $(30 + 10 + m) = (40 + m) \text{ kg}$.
Tension in the upper wire, $T_u = (40 + m)g = (40 + m) \times 10$
To avoid breaking the upper wire:
$(40 + m) \times 10 \le 960$
$40 + m \le 96 \implies m \le 56 \text{ kg}$

For the lower wire:
Area of cross-section, $A_l = 0.004 \text{ cm}^2 = 4 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}^2$
Maximum tension the lower wire can withstand is:
$T_{l,max} = \sigma_{max} \times A_l = (12 \times 10^8) \times (4 \times 10^{-7}) = 480 \text{ N}$
The total mass supported by the lower wire is $(10 + m) \text{ kg}$.
Tension in the lower wire, $T_l = (10 + m)g = (10 + m) \times 10$
To avoid breaking the lower wire:
$(10 + m) \times 10 \le 480$
$10 + m \le 48 \implies m \le 38 \text{ kg}$

To ensure neither wire breaks, the added mass $m$ must satisfy both conditions. Therefore, the maximum mass that can be added is the smaller of the two values:
$m = 38 \text{ kg}$

Answer: $38$

subject: Physics
solution:
The data given is 

Y=7×1010 N m-2 and∆ll=0.04100

Young's modulus is given by the ratio of stress and strain,

Y=FA∆ll=FlA∆l

The energy stored is given as 

E=YA2l∆l2⇒E=YA2∆ll2×l   ...(i)

The energy per unit volume can be written from equation (i)

EV=Y2×∆ll2   ...(ii)

Substituting the values in equation (ii)

EV=12×7×1010×0.04×0.04104=56×102

subject: Physics
solution:
Bulk modulus is given by $B = \dfrac{\Delta P}{-\dfrac{\Delta V}{V}}$

The fractional decrease in volume is $-\dfrac{\Delta V}{V} = \dfrac{\Delta P}{B}$

Substituting the given values:

$-\dfrac{\Delta V}{V} = \dfrac{6.3 \times 10^7}{2.1 \times 10^9} = 3 \times 10^{-2}$

The percentage decrease in volume is $\left(-\dfrac{\Delta V}{V}\right) \times 100$

Percentage decrease $= 3 \times 10^{-2} \times 100 = 3\%$

Answer: $3\%$

subject: Physics
solution:
Let $T$ be the tension in the wire at a distance $\dfrac{l}{3}$ from the top.

The length of the wire below this point is $l - \dfrac{l}{3} = \dfrac{2l}{3}$.

The weight of this lower part of the wire is $\dfrac{w}{l} \times \dfrac{2l}{3} = \dfrac{2w}{3}$.

The total downward force (tension $T$) at this point is the sum of the weight $W$ attached at the bottom and the weight of the lower part of the wire:

$T = W + \dfrac{2w}{3}$

The stress at this point is given by the tension divided by the cross-sectional area $A$:

$\text{Stress} = \dfrac{T}{A} = \dfrac{W + \dfrac{2w}{3}}{A} = \dfrac{W}{A} + \dfrac{2}{3}\dfrac{w}{A}$

Comparing this with the given expression for stress, $\dfrac{W}{A} + \dfrac{2}{\gamma}\dfrac{w}{A}$, we get:

$\gamma = 3$

Answer: $3$

subject: Physics
solution:
Y-Young's modulus, K-Bulk modulus, η-modulus of rigidity
We know that, 

Y=3k1-2σ

⇒σ=121-Y3k           ...1

⇒Y=2η1+σ⇒σ=Y2η-1    ...2

From the equation 1 and the equation 2

121-Y3k=Y2η-1

⇒1-Y3k=Yη-2⇒Y3k=3-Yn⇒Y3k=3η-Yη

⇒ηY3k=3η-Y⇒k=ηY9η-3Y N m-2.

subject: Physics
solution:
Given, Initial pressure of liquid $\left(P_i\right)=1 \mathrm{~atm}$
Final pressure of liquid $\left(\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{f}}\right)=5 \mathrm{~atm}$
Change in pressure $(\mathrm{dP})=\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{f}}-\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{i}}=4 \mathrm{~atm}$
$=4 \times 10^5 \mathrm{~Pa}$
Change in volume $(\mathrm{dV})=-0.8 \mathrm{~cm}^3$
Bulk modulus $(B)=2 \times 10^9 \mathrm{~Pa}$
Now, $B=\frac{-d P}{(d V / V)} \Rightarrow V=-B\left(\frac{d V}{d P}\right)$
$\begin{aligned}
\Rightarrow & V=-2 \times 10^9 \times \frac{\left(-0.8 \times 10^{-6}\right)}{4 \times 10^5} \\ & =4 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~m}^3=4 \text { litre }
\end{aligned}$
